---
title: "Foundations: the registration problem"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
This is rung 0 of the registration unit — the page that turns "I know linear
algebra and I have read the geometry unit" into "I can read the rest of this book
as one story." Three ideas hold up everything that follows:

1. **A registration is a map between two geometries, found by minimizing a
   mismatch energy.** Given a fixed image $I_f$ and a moving image $I_m$, we look
   for a transform $\varphi$ minimizing
   $\mathcal{S}(I_f,\, I_m\circ\varphi) + \lambda\,\mathcal{R}(\varphi)$ —
   *similarity* plus *regularisation* over a *parametrized family of transforms*.
   Those three ingredients are the three things the rest of the unit fills in.
2. **We pull the moving image *back* onto the fixed grid.** The map runs
   $\varphi:\Omega_f\to\Omega_m$, and the warped image is the *composition*
   $I_m\circ\varphi$. This single convention — backward warping, not forward
   splatting — is the most important one in the unit.
3. **The whole engine is pure-functional, on-device, and differentiable
   end-to-end.** Every routine carries an execution class (**DIFF-JAX**,
   **HOST-CTOR**, **HOST-QA**) under one rule (*never a HOST op inside a jitted
   loop*), outputs are `NamedTuple`s of arrays, and a whole registration is
   reverse-differentiable — you can backprop a downstream loss to the moving image
   or to the transform parameters.

If you read only one page before the transform ladder, read this one.
:::

This unit is written for a reader who is comfortable with **linear algebra** —
matrices, least squares, eigendecomposition, gradients, the chain rule — and who
has read the [geometry unit](../geometry/foundations.qmd), so a voxel grid is
already a familiar object: a discrete sampling of a continuous field, with cell
$i$ sitting at position $i$ in **index space**. But the reader has had **no formal
training in image registration, Lie theory, numerical optimization, or
graphics**. Everything here is built up from what you already own. Our aim across
the book is to carry you from *novice* (you can call a recipe and pick the right
one) to *practitioner* (you understand why each answer converges and what each
argument does) to *developer* (you could implement it yourself). This page lays
the four planks the whole climb rests on: the problem, the pull-back convention,
the transform ladder, and the coordinate spaces — plus the execution-class and
validation postures the suite shares with its
[statistics](../statistics/foundations.qmd) and
[geometry](../geometry/foundations.qmd) siblings.

## A motivating question {#sec-motivation}

Hold one concrete task in mind for the entire page. You have a subject's
**T1-weighted anatomical scan** — a 3-D volume of the brain on the scanner's voxel
grid — and you want to put it into **MNI space**, the standard template
coordinate frame that lets you compare this subject to every other subject and to
an atlas. Concretely: you have the subject T1 (call it the **moving** image, the
one we will move) and the MNI template (the **fixed** image, the frame we move
*into*), and you want a transform that, applied to the subject, lands their brain
on top of the template's.

What does "lands on top of" mean, operationally? It means we need three things,
and the whole unit is the elaboration of these three:

- a **family of transforms** rich enough to express the misalignment (a rigid
  pose difference? a global size and shear? a fine, subject-specific folding
  pattern?),
- a way to **score how well** a candidate transform aligns the two images (when
  do two brains "match" — same intensities? same local texture? same information
  content, across modalities?), and
- a way to keep the transform **physical** (a real brain does not tear or fold
  inside-out), and a way to **search** the family for the best-scoring transform.

We will meet all three on this page in outline and devote a later page to each. By
the end you will see that aligning a T1 to MNI is one instance of a single
template: *minimize a mismatch energy over a parametrized family of maps between
two geometries.*

## The registration problem {#sec-problem}

Let us write the question down. We are given a **fixed** image $I_f$ (the MNI
template) defined over a grid $\Omega_f$, and a **moving** image $I_m$ (the subject
T1) defined over a grid $\Omega_m$. Both are just intensities over voxels — real
numbers indexed by integer coordinates. We want a transform $\varphi$ that brings
the moving image into agreement with the fixed one.

The obvious first thing to ask is: *agreement, measured how?* Suppose for a moment
the two images are the same modality (both T1) and have the same intensity scale.
Then the most direct measure of disagreement is the **sum of squared differences**
between the fixed image and the moving image *after we have applied $\varphi$* —
add up, over every voxel, the squared gap between what the fixed image shows there
and what the warped moving image shows there. The smaller that sum, the better the
alignment. Call this number the **similarity** (really a *dis*similarity — lower is
better):

$$
\mathcal{S}\big(I_f,\; I_m\circ\varphi\big)
  \;=\; \sum_{x\in\Omega_f} \big(I_f(x) - (I_m\circ\varphi)(x)\big)^2 .
$$ {#eq-ssd}

Here $x$ ranges over the fixed grid, $I_f(x)$ is the fixed intensity at voxel $x$,
and $(I_m\circ\varphi)(x)$ is the **warped moving** image — the moving image
*resampled* at the transformed coordinate $\varphi(x)$ (the next section earns the
composition $I_m\circ\varphi$ in full). @eq-ssd is the **SSD** metric, the
within-modality workhorse; `nitrix` ships it as `register.SSD`, and three more —
`LNCC` (local normalized cross-correlation, robust to a smooth intensity bias),
`MI` (mutual information) and `CorrelationRatio` (both cross-modal) — for the cases
where intensities do not match voxel-for-voxel. They are all
[similarity metrics](similarity.qmd#sec-ssd); the choice is *what counts as
agreement*, and it is the subject of an entire page.

So a first attempt at the registration problem is: *find the $\varphi$ that
minimizes @eq-ssd.* This is the right idea, and it breaks in two ways that motivate
the other two ingredients.

**It breaks if the family of transforms is too free.** If we let $\varphi$ be
*any* map from grid to grid — an arbitrary displacement at every voxel — then we
can drive @eq-ssd to zero by tearing the moving image into confetti and pasting
each fixed voxel's exact intensity wherever it happens to occur. That "perfect"
alignment is physical nonsense: a brain does not fold inside-out or tear. The cure
is one idea: add a **regulariser** $\mathcal{R}(\varphi)$ that scores how *physical*
the transform is — large when $\varphi$ is jagged, folded, or non-smooth, small
when it is the kind of smooth, invertible deformation a real anatomy could undergo.
We then minimize the *sum*

$$
\mathcal{E}(\varphi)
  \;=\; \underbrace{\mathcal{S}\big(I_f,\; I_m\circ\varphi\big)}_{\text{data term: match the images}}
  \;+\; \lambda\,\underbrace{\mathcal{R}(\varphi)}_{\text{regulariser: keep it physical}} ,
$$ {#eq-energy}

where $\lambda\ge 0$ trades the two off: $\lambda=0$ chases the data at any cost,
large $\lambda$ insists on smoothness even at the price of a worse intensity match.
@eq-energy is the **governing equation of the entire unit** — every page is one
choice of $\mathcal{S}$, one family for $\varphi$, one $\mathcal{R}$, and one way to
do the minimization. `nitrix` ships the regularisers the
[diffeomorphic page](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-regularisers) consumes —
`gradient_smoothness` (penalize the displacement's first derivatives),
`bending_energy` (penalize its second derivatives, the thin-plate energy), and
`jacobian_folding_penalty` (punish a Jacobian determinant that drops toward zero,
i.e. an incipient fold) — all in `register.regulariser`.

**It breaks if the family is too restricted *and* we look for $\varphi$ naïvely.**
Even a rich-enough family does not hand you the optimum: @eq-energy is a
*non-convex* function of $\varphi$'s parameters, with one good basin around the true
alignment and false basins beside it. @fig-foundations-objective makes this
concrete with the simplest possible family — a single translation. Slide the moving
profile left/right and plot the SSD: there is a deep **capture-range basin** around
the true shift, flanked by **local minima** where a structure has slid one feature
over and locked onto the wrong neighbour. An optimiser started outside the central
basin rolls into a trap. This is why registration is *optimization*, not a formula,
and why the [optimization page](optimization.qmd#sec-coarse-fine) leans on a
coarse-to-fine pyramid (blur away the side basins first) and a good starting guess
to keep the search inside the right basin.

```{python}
#| label: fig-foundations-objective
#| fig-cap: "The registration objective, on the simplest family there is — a single translation. Left: a 1-D intensity profile (two bright structures) as the fixed image $I_f$, and the same profile shifted as the moving image; the grey shading is the per-voxel mismatch the data term sums. Right: the SSD objective $\\mathcal{S}(I_f, I_m\\circ\\varphi_\\theta)$ as the translation parameter $\\theta$ sweeps. There is one deep capture-range basin around the true shift (green), and two local minima beside it (orange) where a structure has locked onto the wrong neighbour. Registration is the search for the global basin; an optimiser started in a side trap converges to a wrong answer — the reason for coarse-to-fine and a good initial guess."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# A 1-D intensity profile standing in for one scan-line of an image: two
# Gaussian "structures" (two bright nuclei) on a flat background.
n = 256
x = np.arange(n)
def profile(a):
    g1 = np.exp(-0.5 * ((x - (96 + a)) / 9.0) ** 2)
    g2 = 0.7 * np.exp(-0.5 * ((x - (150 + a)) / 14.0) ** 2)
    return g1 + g2

fixed = profile(0.0)
trials = np.linspace(-90, 90, 721)
ssd = np.array([np.sum((profile(a) - fixed) ** 2) for a in trials])

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.4))
axL.plot(x, fixed, color='#3182bd', lw=2, label=r'fixed  $I_f$')
axL.plot(x, profile(34.0), color='#e6550d', lw=2,
         label=r'moving  $I_m\circ\varphi$  (shifted)')
axL.fill_between(x, fixed, profile(34.0), color='#999999', alpha=0.25,
                 label='per-voxel mismatch')
axL.set_xlabel('voxel index along a scan-line')
axL.set_ylabel('intensity')
axL.set_title('the data term scores image mismatch', fontsize=10)
axL.legend(fontsize=8, loc='upper right')

axR.plot(trials, ssd, color='#333333', lw=1.8)
gmin = trials[np.argmin(ssd)]
axR.axvline(gmin, color='#31a354', ls='--', lw=1.3,
            label=f'global minimum (a = {gmin:.0f})')
d = np.diff(ssd); sign = np.sign(d)
maxima = np.where((sign[:-1] > 0) & (sign[1:] < 0))[0] + 1
minima = np.where((sign[:-1] < 0) & (sign[1:] > 0))[0] + 1
gi = int(np.argmin(ssd))
lo = maxima[maxima < gi].max() if np.any(maxima < gi) else 0
hi = maxima[maxima > gi].min() if np.any(maxima > gi) else len(trials) - 1
axR.axvspan(trials[lo], trials[hi], color='#31a354', alpha=0.12,
            label='capture-range basin')
for mi in minima:
    if mi != gi:
        axR.plot(trials[mi], ssd[mi], 'o', color='#e6550d', ms=6)
axR.plot([], [], 'o', color='#e6550d', ms=6, label='local minima (false optima)')
axR.set_xlabel(r'translation parameter $\theta$  (voxels)')
axR.set_ylabel(r'$\mathcal{S}(I_f,\, I_m\circ\varphi_\theta)$  (SSD)')
axR.set_title('the objective: one basin to fall into, traps beside it', fontsize=10)
axR.legend(fontsize=8, loc='upper center')
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**The three ingredients, named.** Pulling the strands together, every registration
in `nitrix` is built from exactly three things, and the architecture mirrors them:

| Ingredient | What it is | Where it lives |
|---|---|---|
| **Transform family** | the parametrized set $\varphi$ ranges over (rigid, affine, a velocity field, a sphere map) | a `TransformModel` (`Rigid` / `Affine`) for the matrix recipes; a velocity/displacement field for the diffeomorphic ones |
| **Similarity** $\mathcal{S}$ | what counts as agreement | a `Metric` (`SSD` / `LNCC` / `MI` / `CorrelationRatio`) |
| **Regulariser** $\mathcal{R}$ | what keeps $\varphi$ physical | `gradient_smoothness` / `bending_energy` / `jacobian_folding_penalty` (and the implicit smoothness of a constrained family) |

These three plug into a shared **driver** — `register.multi_resolution_register`,
configured by a `RegistrationSpec` (pyramid depth `levels`, per-level `iterations`,
the `metric`, the `interpolation` kernel) — that does the coarse-to-fine
optimization once and is reused by every matrix recipe. The output is a
`RegistrationResult`: the recovered `matrix`, its raw `params`, the `warped` moving
image, and the optimiser `cost_history`. For the T1→MNI task, the call is
`affine_register(subject_t1, mni_template, spec=RegistrationSpec(levels=3))`, and
`result.warped` is the subject's brain resampled onto the template grid.

## Pull-back: why we warp backward, not forward {#sec-pullback}

Now we earn the composition $I_m\circ\varphi$ — the single most important
convention in the unit, and the one most worth slowing down for.

We have a transform $\varphi$ and a moving image $I_m$, and we want the **warped**
image: $I_m$ "moved" by $\varphi$, sitting on the fixed grid so we can compare it to
$I_f$ voxel-by-voxel. There are two ways a newcomer might compute this, and only one
of them works.

**The obvious thing (forward splatting), and where it breaks.** The intuitive
picture is: *take each pixel of the moving image, figure out where it goes, and
paint it there.* For moving pixel at position $p$, compute its destination
$\psi(p)$, round to the nearest fixed-grid cell, and write the intensity. This is
**forward splatting** (or *scattering*). It seems natural — it is how you would move
furniture — and it fails on a grid. The destinations $\psi(p)$ are real-valued and
almost never land exactly on a fixed-grid cell; worse, when $\varphi$ magnifies a
region, adjacent moving pixels land *more than one cell apart*, so the warped image
is riddled with **holes** — fixed-grid cells that no moving pixel happened to hit.
And where $\varphi$ shrinks a region, several moving pixels pile into one cell and
you have to decide how to combine them. @fig-foundations-pullback (middle panel)
shows the carnage: a clean disk and square, splatted forward under a modest
rotation-and-magnification, come out as a red lattice of cracks. You could patch the
holes with a scatter-and-normalize or a painter's pass, but it is fragile, it is
not differentiable in a clean way, and there is a better idea.

**The cure (backward pull-back), and why it is dense.** Turn the question around.
Instead of asking *where does each moving pixel go?*, ask, for each **fixed-grid**
cell $x$, *where did it come from in the moving image?* — and read the value there.
That destination-to-source map is exactly $\varphi$: it takes a fixed coordinate
$x\in\Omega_f$ and returns the moving coordinate $\varphi(x)\in\Omega_m$ it should be
filled from. We then sample $I_m$ at $\varphi(x)$ — interpolating between the
moving image's voxels, since $\varphi(x)$ is real-valued — and that interpolated
value is the warped intensity. Because we *visit every fixed cell* and *always find
a source*, the result is **dense**: no holes, ever (@fig-foundations-pullback, right
panel). This is **backward warping**, and writing it as function composition makes
the whole thing one line:

$$
(I_m\circ\varphi)(x) \;=\; I_m\big(\varphi(x)\big),
\qquad \varphi:\Omega_f \to \Omega_m .
$$ {#eq-pullback}

Read @eq-pullback aloud: *the warped image at the fixed cell $x$ is the moving image
sampled at $\varphi(x)$.* The map direction is the crux — and it is **the
convention for the whole unit**:

::: {.callout-important title="The map-direction convention"}
$\varphi$ maps the **fixed** grid to the **moving** grid: $\varphi:\Omega_f\to
\Omega_m$. It tells each fixed-grid cell where to *pull from* in the moving image.
The warped moving image is the composition $I_m\circ\varphi$. We register by
**pulling the moving image back onto the fixed grid**, never by splatting it
forward. Everything downstream — the sign of the recovered matrix, the way warps
compose, the resampling kernels — follows from this one choice.
:::

In `nitrix`, @eq-pullback is `geometry.spatial_transform(image, deformation)`: it
takes the moving `image` and an **absolute coordinate grid** `deformation` (the
array of $\varphi(x)$ values, one per fixed cell) and returns the pulled-back image,
interpolating with a chosen `Interpolator` (linear by default). The coordinate grid
itself is built by `geometry.affine_grid(matrix, fixed_shape)` for a matrix
transform, or as `geometry.identity_grid(fixed_shape) + u` for a displacement field
$u$ (note the convention: a *displacement* $u$ is **relative**, $\varphi(x)=x+u(x)$;
the array `spatial_transform` consumes is the **absolute** grid `identity_grid + u`).
The mechanics of that interpolation — the kernels, the out-of-bounds boundary modes,
the separable fast paths — are the subject of the
[resampling page](resampling.qmd#sec-pullback-sampling); here the only thing to
carry forward is the *direction*. **DIFF-JAX:** `spatial_transform`, `affine_grid`,
and `apply_affine` are all jittable and differentiable in both the image and the
coordinates (with the linear-interpolation kernel), which is what later lets a whole
registration carry a gradient.

```{python}
#| label: fig-foundations-pullback
#| fig-cap: "Why we resample by pull-back. Left: a small synthetic moving image (a disk and a square). Middle: forward splatting — push each moving pixel to its destination on the fixed grid and write it. Under a rotation-and-magnification the destinations spread apart, so the warped body is shot through with interior holes (red): cells no moving pixel landed on. Right: backward pull-back — for each fixed cell, sample the moving image at $\\varphi(x)$ and interpolate. Every output cell is filled; the warp is dense and clean. This is why $\\varphi$ runs fixed→moving and the warped image is the composition $I_m\\circ\\varphi$."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# A small synthetic image: a 2-D phantom (a bright disk + a square) on a grid.
N = 48
yy, xx = np.mgrid[0:N, 0:N]
img = np.zeros((N, N))
img[(xx - 18) ** 2 + (yy - 18) ** 2 < 10 ** 2] = 1.0          # disk
img[28:42, 26:42] = 0.6                                       # square
cx = cy = (N - 1) / 2.0

# phi maps a fixed-grid coordinate -> a moving-grid coordinate: a rotation +
# magnification about the centre.  The magnification (fixed sampled denser than
# the moving grid) is exactly the regime where forward splatting tears.
theta = np.deg2rad(18.0)
mag = 1.6
c, sn = np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)
A_phi = (1.0 / mag) * np.array([[c, -sn], [sn, c]])           # fixed -> moving

def phi(coords):
    p = coords - np.array([cy, cx])
    return p @ A_phi.T + np.array([cy, cx])

# FORWARD SPLATTING: push each moving pixel forward (moving -> fixed = the
# inverse of phi), round to nearest, write.  Under magnification the warped
# body is riddled with interior holes.
forward = np.zeros((N, N)); hit = np.zeros((N, N), dtype=bool)
A_fwd = np.linalg.inv(A_phi)
for r in range(N):
    for cc in range(N):
        if img[r, cc] == 0:
            continue
        dst = (np.array([r, cc], float) - [cy, cx]) @ A_fwd.T + [cy, cx]
        ri, ci = int(round(dst[0])), int(round(dst[1]))
        if 0 <= ri < N and 0 <= ci < N:
            forward[ri, ci] = img[r, cc]; hit[ri, ci] = True

# BACKWARD PULL-BACK: for each fixed cell, sample the moving image at phi(x)
# bilinearly.  Every output cell is filled.
fgrid = np.stack([yy, xx], axis=-1).astype(float)
src = phi(fgrid)
r0 = np.floor(src[..., 0]).astype(int); c0 = np.floor(src[..., 1]).astype(int)
dr = src[..., 0] - r0; dc = src[..., 1] - c0
inb = ((src[..., 0] >= 0) & (src[..., 0] <= N - 1)
       & (src[..., 1] >= 0) & (src[..., 1] <= N - 1))
def gather(rr, cc):
    return img[np.clip(rr, 0, N - 1), np.clip(cc, 0, N - 1)] * inb
backward = (gather(r0, c0) * (1 - dr) * (1 - dc) + gather(r0 + 1, c0) * dr * (1 - dc)
            + gather(r0, c0 + 1) * (1 - dr) * dc + gather(r0 + 1, c0 + 1) * dr * dc)

warped_body = backward > 0.05
interior_holes = warped_body & (~hit)
n_interior = int(interior_holes.sum())

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11.5, 4.0))
axes[0].imshow(img, cmap='gray', vmin=0, vmax=1)
axes[0].set_title('moving image $I_m$', fontsize=10)
axes[1].imshow(forward, cmap='gray', vmin=0, vmax=1)
overlay = np.zeros((N, N, 4)); overlay[interior_holes] = [0.9, 0.1, 0.1, 0.85]
axes[1].imshow(overlay)
axes[1].set_title(f'forward splat: {n_interior} interior holes (red)', fontsize=10)
axes[2].imshow(backward, cmap='gray', vmin=0, vmax=1)
axes[2].set_title('backward pull-back: dense', fontsize=10)
for ax in axes:
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

One corollary worth stating now, because it surprises people: the matrix a recipe
*recovers* maps fixed→moving, not moving→fixed. `result.matrix` from
`rigid_register` / `affine_register` is the **fixed-voxel → moving-voxel** map — the
$\varphi$ of @eq-pullback as a homogeneous matrix — precisely so that
`apply_transform(image, result)` can pull any moving-frame image (a second contrast,
a label map, an atlas ROI) straight onto the fixed grid by the same composition. It
feels backward until you remember we resample by pulling back.

## The transform ladder {#sec-ladder}

We said the first ingredient is a *family* of transforms. There is not one family;
there is a **ladder** of them, from rigid at the bottom to dense diffeomorphic and
surface maps at the top, and the unit climbs it rung by rung. Each rung answers the
same four questions — how is the transform *represented*, *applied*, *scored*, and
*found* — but trades off three quantities: **degrees of freedom** (how many numbers
parametrize $\varphi$), **flexibility** (how much misalignment it can express), and
**regularisation need** (how badly it overfits without a $\mathcal{R}$ to hold it
back). More freedom buys more flexibility *and* more ways to go wrong.

The obvious place to start is the most constrained transform that could possibly
align two brains, and to add freedom only when it fails:

- **Rigid** — rotation plus translation, nothing else. The transform is an element
  of the special Euclidean group $SE(d)$, with **6 DOF** in 3-D (three rotation
  angles, three translations) and **3 DOF** in 2-D. It preserves all distances and
  angles: a rigid map can reposition a brain but cannot stretch it. This is the
  right rung for **within-subject** alignment, where the object truly is the same
  size and shape and only its *pose* differs — realigning the frames of an fMRI
  series to correct head motion, or aligning two scans of the same person from
  different sessions. `nitrix` parametrizes it as `register.Rigid`
  ($n_\text{params} = 6$ in 3-D), with the recipe `rigid_register` and the batched
  motion-correction recipe `volreg`. Six numbers cannot overfit a brain's worth of
  voxels, so a rigid fit needs *no* explicit regulariser — the constraint *is* the
  regulariser.

- **Affine** — a general linear map plus translation: rotation, but now also
  anisotropic scale and shear. The transform lives in $\mathrm{Aff}(d)$, with
  **12 DOF** in 3-D (a $3\times3$ linear block plus a 3-vector) and **6 DOF** in
  2-D. It can resize and skew, which is exactly what you need for **cross-subject
  global normalization**: different people's brains differ in overall size and
  proportion, and an affine soaks up that gross difference before any fine warping.
  This is the workhorse first stage of T1→MNI. `nitrix` ships it as
  `register.Affine` ($n_\text{params}=12$ in 3-D) and `affine_register`. Twelve
  numbers still cannot overfit, so affine too is self-regularising; the standard
  recipe warm-starts affine from a rigid fit (a staged pipeline,
  [`syn_pipeline`](recipes.qmd#sec-specs)).

- **Diffeomorphic** — a smooth, invertible, fold-free deformation with a
  *per-voxel* displacement. This is where the DOF count explodes: the transform is
  represented by a **velocity field** $v(x)$ (one vector per voxel) whose flow
  exponential $\varphi=\exp(v)$ guarantees invertibility, so the parameter count is
  *millions* — three numbers per voxel over a whole volume. That freedom is what
  captures **subject-specific folding** (the part of cross-subject alignment an
  affine cannot reach: this person's central sulcus onto the template's), and it is
  also what makes the regulariser non-negotiable: with three numbers per voxel you
  *can* tear the brain, so $\mathcal{R}(\varphi)$ — smoothness of the field, a
  positive Jacobian determinant — is the only thing keeping the map physical.
  `nitrix` ships two recipes here, `diffeomorphic_demons_register` (log-domain
  Demons, a stationary velocity field) and `greedy_syn_register` (symmetric,
  LNCC-driven, SyN-style), both detailed on the
  [diffeomorphic page](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-svf).

- **Surface** — a map between *cortical sheets*, parametrized on the **sphere**.
  When the object is the cortex represented as a triangle mesh (from the geometry
  unit), the natural domain is not a volume grid at all but the unit sphere onto
  which the cortex inflates, and "registration" means rotating/warping one
  subject's spherical folding pattern onto a template sphere (fsaverage). The
  representation, resampling, and convolution all move onto the sphere; this is the
  top of the ladder, on the [surface page](surface-registration.qmd#sec-parameterize).

The shape of the trade-off is worth keeping in a single mental image, summarized
here:

| Rung | Family | DOF (3-D) | Suits | Regulariser |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rigid | $SE(3)$ | 6 | within-subject pose (motion, sessions) | none needed (constraint *is* the regulariser) |
| Affine | $\mathrm{Aff}(3)$ | 12 | cross-subject *global* size/shape | none needed |
| Diffeomorphic | velocity field $v$ | $\sim$ millions | cross-subject *local* folding | essential (smoothness + $\det J>0$) |
| Surface | sphere map | many | cortex onto fsaverage | essential (spherical smoothness) |

The cross-modal axis is orthogonal to the ladder: aligning an EPI to its own T1
(same subject, different *modality*) is usually a **rigid** problem (same head,
different contrast) — what changes is not the rung but the *similarity*, swapping
SSD for `MI` or `CorrelationRatio`, since the two images do not share an intensity
scale. So the rung is chosen by *how the geometry differs* (pose vs size vs
folding), and the metric by *how the intensities relate* (same modality vs cross).
The [transforms page](transforms.qmd#sec-homogeneous) opens the matrix rungs in
full; this page only asks you to hold the ladder in mind.

## Coordinate spaces: index vs world {#sec-spaces}

We have been loose about *what coordinates $\varphi$ acts on*. There are two honest
answers, and the choice between them is the one axis that separates a fast,
shared-grid registration from a physically correct one. `nitrix` factors it behind a
small abstraction — the `CoordinateSpace` (in `register._space`), a `Protocol` with
two implementers, `IndexSpace` and `WorldSpace` — so that the driver, the pyramid,
and the per-level optimise are written *once* and only this one axis varies.

**`IndexSpace` — the shared voxel grid.** The simplest answer is that $\varphi$ acts
directly in **voxel-index coordinates**: voxel $i$ is at position $i$, and the
transform maps fixed indices to moving indices with no detour. This is fast — the
sampling matrix is just `model.exp(params)`, no general matrix inverse is taken, so
the whole path stays **on-device** (no fallback to a host linear solver) — and it is
correct *when the two images share a voxel grid*, the norm for within-modality work
like motion correction. `IndexSpace` is the default. Its one assumption is the
shared grid: under it, the parameter vector is in *voxel units*, so when the
coarse-to-fine driver warm-starts a finer pyramid level it must rescale the
translation block to the new grid (`requires_grid_rescale = True`).
**Execution class:** a `dataclass(frozen=True)`, hashable, riding `jit` static args;
its samplers are pure DIFF-JAX matrix builders.

**`WorldSpace` — physical millimetres.** The shared-grid assumption breaks for real
inter-image registration. A subject T1 and the MNI template live on *different*
grids with *different* voxel sizes, and clinical scans have **anisotropic** voxels
(say $0.9\times0.9\times4$ mm). A rotation expressed in index space on an
anisotropic grid is *not* a rotation in physical space — it *shears* — so an
index-space "rigid" fit returns a physically wrong transform, silently. The cure is
to carry, for each image, its **voxel→world affine** ($A_f$ for fixed, $A_m$ for
moving) — the matrix the scanner records (a NIfTI `sform`) that maps voxel indices
to physical millimetres — and to do the optimization in world coordinates. The
sampling map that pulls a fixed voxel back to a moving voxel then composes three
steps:

$$
\underbrace{A_m^{-1}}_{\text{world}\to\text{moving voxel}}
\;\cdot\;
\underbrace{T_\text{world}}_{\text{the fitted world}\to\text{world map}}
\;\cdot\;
\underbrace{A_f}_{\text{fixed voxel}\to\text{world}} .
$$ {#eq-worldcompose}

Read @eq-worldcompose right-to-left, following a fixed voxel: $A_f$ lifts it to
physical space, the fitted transform $T_\text{world}$ moves it, and $A_m^{-1}$ drops
it onto the moving voxel grid. This is correct under anisotropy and across differing
grids. It costs exactly **one** matrix inverse — a single `safe_inv` of the $4\times4$
moving affine, taken *once* per registration, not in the hot loop. Because the world
parameters are physical quantities independent of any grid, no inter-level rescale is
needed (`requires_grid_rescale = False`). For the T1→MNI task with real anisotropic
data, this is the correct space:
`affine_register(t1, mni, space=WorldSpace(fixed_affine=A_mni, moving_affine=A_t1))`.
**Execution class:** `WorldSpace` is a `dataclass(frozen=True)` carrying the two
affines, but binding it to a registration is **HOST-CTOR** — its `sampler(...)` takes
the `safe_inv` of the moving affine (which may fall back to a host solver), so that
inverse is resolved *once*, before the jitted loop, into a concrete matrix the
DIFF-JAX samplers reuse at every level.

**Centering: transforms act about the image centre.** Both spaces share a subtle but
load-bearing choice: the rotation/scaling is applied **about the image centre, not
the voxel origin** (the corner). The reason is *conditioning*. If you rotate about a
far corner, a one-degree rotation moves the opposite side of the brain by a huge
distance, so the rotation and translation parameters become wildly different in
scale and the optimiser's job is ill-conditioned. Rotating about the centre keeps
the parameters comparable and the basin well-shaped. Concretely, `affine_grid`
defaults its `center` to the grid centre $(\text{shape}-1)/2$, and applies the
transform as `apply_affine`'s $\,M\,(x-c) + t + c\,$ — subtract the centre, transform,
add it back.

**Self-contained result matrices: the centre is baked in.** Here is the elegant part.
The optimiser works with a transform *about the origin* (`model.exp(params)`), but
the warp applies it *about the centre*. If the returned matrix carried only the
about-origin parameters, every consumer would have to re-derive the centre to use it.
Instead, both spaces return a **self-contained** `result.matrix`: the centre is baked
in by the conjugation $\,\mathrm{Tr}(c)\cdot \mathbf{A}\cdot\mathrm{Tr}(-c)\,$ (the
internal `_conjugate_about`), so that `apply_affine(coords, result.matrix)` reproduces
the warp *directly*, about the origin, with the centring already folded in — and two
such matrices **compose** by plain multiplication without re-deriving anything. (The
raw about-origin Lie coordinates remain available on `result.params` for anyone who
needs them.) This is what lets an `IndexSpace` rigid result and an affine result
multiply cleanly in a staged pipeline, and what makes `result.matrix` a portable
artifact you can save and reapply. In `IndexSpace` the self-contained matrix is the
full-resolution fixed-voxel→moving-voxel index map; in `WorldSpace` it is the
fixed-world→moving-world transform in millimetres.

## Execution classes and transparent differentiability {#sec-classes}

The registration suite shares the geometry unit's **execution-class** discipline.
Every public routine is one of three kinds, and the classification is operational,
not bureaucratic: it tells you *whether you can call the routine inside a jitted,
differentiated optimisation loop.*

- **DIFF-JAX** — jittable *and* differentiable, pure array math with shapes fixed at
  trace time; safe inside a `jit` / `vmap` / `grad` / `lax.scan`. The hot path of
  every recipe is DIFF-JAX: `spatial_transform`, `affine_grid`, `apply_affine`,
  `rigid_exp` / `affine_exp` (the transform charts), the metric `cost` / `residual`
  evaluations, the matrix-free Gauss–Newton / LM / BFGS optimiser steps, the
  scaling-and-squaring velocity exponential. These are exactly the routines written
  to stay on-device (closed-form small matrix work, matrix-free solves) so the loop
  never touches a fragile dense GPU solver.
- **HOST-CTOR** — host-side *construction* whose output shape is data-dependent and
  so cannot be traced: building a `WorldSpace` sampler from a pair of affines (it
  takes a `safe_inv` that may fall back to the host), pinning a histogram metric's
  intensity range from the full-resolution images, resolving a moment-based initial
  transform. These run *before* the jitted loop, producing concrete arrays the loop
  consumes.
- **HOST-QA** — host-side *query* returning a Python scalar/bool, off the
  differentiable path: a folding check `float(result.jacobian_det.min()) > 0`, a
  convergence test on a returned cost history. These run *after* the loop.

::: {.callout-important title="The load-bearing rule"}
**A HOST op (CTOR or QA) must never be called inside a jitted loop.** The metric
range is pinned *once* on the host before the coarse-to-fine loop (precisely so that
`volreg` can `vmap` the per-frame core, where an eager `float(moving.min())` cannot
run); the `WorldSpace` inverse is taken *once* before the loop; the diffeomorphism QA
runs *after* it. The discipline is the same one the geometry unit states: construction
and query bracket the loop; only DIFF-JAX lives inside it.
:::

**Pure-functional outputs.** The recipes are *pure functions* of `(moving, fixed)`
with a `jit`-static `spec`; they return `NamedTuple`s of plain arrays
(`RegistrationResult`, `DiffeomorphicResult`, `SyNResult`, `VolregResult`,
`BBRResult`) — *no* PyTree modules, no stateful objects, no I/O. This is the
`reml_fit` precedent from the statistics unit: a result is data, not an object with
methods. It is what lets you `jit` a whole registration, `vmap` it over a cohort, and
reason about it as a mathematical function.

**The transparent-differentiability story.** Because the entire hot path is DIFF-JAX
and the outputs are plain arrays, a whole registration is **differentiable
end-to-end**. With the default fixed-iteration mode (`mode='fixed'`, a reproducible
`lax.scan`), `jax.grad` through a recipe "just works" out of the box: every primitive
— the warp, the metric, the optimiser step — is differentiable, so you can place a
registration inside a loss and backpropagate a downstream objective all the way to
the **moving image**, the **fixed image**, or the **transform parameters**. (`mode`
is a field on the `RegistrationSpec`, defaulting to `'fixed'`, so out of the box every
recipe takes this reproducible, reverse-differentiable scan path.) That is
exactly what a differentiable-imaging backend needs: a registrator as a layer, not a
black box. For efficiency at convergence the suite also offers an *implicit* gradient
— `linalg.implicit_least_squares` (the Gauss–Newton-Hessian adjoint, for the
least-squares SSD path) and `linalg.implicit_minimize` (the exact-Hessian adjoint, for
the BFGS metric path) — which differentiate the *optimum* directly, in $O(1)$ memory in
the iteration count, rather than unrolling the trajectory.

One honest caveat, verified in the code. The recipes also offer an **early-exit** mode
(`mode='early_exit'`, a data-dependent `lax.while_loop` that stops when the cost slope
flattens). That mode is **not** reverse-differentiable — a data-dependent trip count
cannot be back-propagated — and `jax.grad` through it raises a *loud, actionable* error
(`register._converge`) pointing you at the implicit entry points or the fixed-scan mode.
The default on every recipe is `mode='fixed'`, so differentiability is the out-of-box
behaviour; early-exit is an opt-in for speed where you do not need a gradient. (A
proposed `custom_vjp` wrapper that would make even the early-exit forward transparently
differentiable via the at-convergence adjoint is *filed but not yet implemented* — see
`docs/feature-requests/registration-recipe-transparent-differentiability.md`; today the
two honest options are fixed-mode autodiff or the explicit implicit combinators.)

## Validation posture: synthetic recovery, not bit-parity {#sec-validation}

How do we know a registration is *correct*? The unit takes the same stance its
[geometry](../geometry/foundations.qmd#sec-validation) and statistics siblings do:
validate by **class and correlation against an oracle, never by bit-parity against
another tool.** This frames every "validated" claim in the unit, so it is worth being
precise — and the brief asks us to verify the scope claims against the code, because
some of the package's own notes are stale.

**ANTs-*style*, not an ANTs clone.** The algorithms are the established lineage — the
`3dvolreg`/`mcflirt` rigid realignment, the AIR-style affine, log-domain Demons,
greedy SyN, boundary-based registration (Greve–Fischl) — and the suite reproduces their
*behaviour*, not their bytes. There is no expectation that `nitrix` and
`antsRegistration` produce the same transform to the last bit: they make different but
legitimate modelling choices (different interpolation, different optimiser, different
regulariser discretisation), so two correct programs will *not* agree bit-for-bit.
Demanding that they do would be testing the arbitrary part, not the real part.

**Synthetic-recovery-validated.** The real part is: *can the recipe recover a known
transform?* The test plants a synthetic warp on an image — a known rigid pose, a known
affine, a known smooth diffeomorphic field — registers the warped image back to the
original, and checks that the recovered transform *undoes* the planted one. The
acceptance gates are quantitative and behavioural: a high similarity after alignment
(normalized cross-correlation $\gtrsim 0.97$–$0.99$ for the rigid/affine recipes, MI
for cross-modal plants), and for the diffeomorphic recipes a **positive Jacobian
determinant everywhere** ($\det J > 0$, i.e. the recovered map does not fold) — the
defining property of a diffeomorphism. There are also internal *parity oracles* that
are exact by construction: a closed-form analytic warp Jacobian checked against
`jax.jacfwd` (same computation, two ways), and the fast inverse-compositional path
checked against the slower forward path it is meant to match. Those are bit-level
checks of *one routine against another routine in the same library* — a different thing
from cross-tool bit-parity.

**Be honest about scope — and check the code, because the docstring is stale.** The
`register.__init__` docstring's "Scope & validation" section lists features as "not yet
implemented." The contract warns that these notes drift, and the code confirms it. The
following are listed as missing but **do in fact ship** (verified in source):

- **Intensity winsorization and histogram matching** — the docstring says "not yet
  implemented," but `register._preprocess.preprocess_images` implements both, and
  `rigid_register` / `affine_register` expose them as the `winsorize=(lo, hi)` and
  `histogram_match=True` arguments (the fMRIPrep front-end). They condition the
  *estimate*, so `warped` is the conditioned moving image; reapply `result.matrix` to
  the original for original intensities.
- **Multi-metric summation (`SumForce`)** — listed as a "not yet" item, but `SumForce`
  *is* exported from `register` and implemented in `register._force`.
- **The closed-form Mattes MI force (`MIForce`)** — the docstring itself notes (and the
  code confirms) that this *is* shipped, as the fast cross-modal diffeomorphic path; the
  correlation-ratio fast force is the one that is genuinely deferred (CR drives the
  diffeomorphic recipes only via the generic autodiff `MetricForce` escape hatch).

What is *genuinely* still open, per both the code and the design record
(`docs/design/registration.md`): **real-data parity vs AFNI/FSL/ANTs on actual volumes**
and the iso-accuracy wall-clock comparison are **delegated to a separate perf-bench
agent** and are not asserted in this repository. So the posture, stated cleanly: the
suite is synthetic-recovery-validated against analytic oracles and internal parity
checks; it is ANTs-*style* in lineage; it is *not* a bit-for-bit `antsRegistration`
clone, and the cross-tool real-data comparison lives elsewhere. When you read or write a
test in this unit, ask *which oracle* — a planted synthetic warp it must recover (assert
high NCC/MI and $\det J>0$), or an internal parity check (assert two routes agree) —
never bit-parity with an external tool.

## Further reading

- Modersitzki, J. (2004). *Numerical Methods for Image Registration.* Oxford
  University Press. The variational formulation behind @eq-energy — data term plus
  regulariser — and the optimisation viewpoint.
- Sotiras, A., Davatzikos, C., & Paragios, N. (2013). *Deformable Medical Image
  Registration: A Survey.* IEEE Transactions on Medical Imaging, 32(7). The map of the
  whole field — the transform ladder, similarity metrics, and regularisers in one place.
- Cox, R. W., & Jesmanowicz, A. (1999). *Real-time 3D image registration for functional
  MRI.* Magnetic Resonance in Medicine, 42(6). The `3dvolreg` rigid/affine
  Gauss–Newton lineage the matrix recipes follow.
- Avants, B. B., Epstein, C. L., Grossman, M., & Gee, J. C. (2008). *Symmetric
  diffeomorphic image registration with cross-correlation.* Medical Image Analysis,
  12(1). SyN — the diffeomorphic quality benchmark the greedy recipe approaches.
- Vercauteren, T., Pennec, X., Perchant, A., & Ayache, N. (2009). *Diffeomorphic demons:
  efficient non-parametric image registration.* NeuroImage, 45(1, Supp. 1). Log-domain
  Demons — the stationary-velocity-field recipe this unit ships.
- Baker, S., & Matthews, I. (2004). *Lucas–Kanade 20 years on: a unifying framework.*
  International Journal of Computer Vision, 56(3). The inverse-compositional fast path
  and the constant-template Hessian behind the rigid/affine optimiser.

## See also

- Source — the coordinate spaces (`IndexSpace` / `WorldSpace`, centering, the
  self-contained result matrix):
  [`src/nitrix/register/_space.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_space.py).
- Source — the registration package surface (the public API this page surveys):
  [`src/nitrix/register/__init__.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/__init__.py).
- Source — the coarse-to-fine driver, `RegistrationSpec`, and `RegistrationResult`:
  [`src/nitrix/register/_core.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_core.py).
- Source — the transform charts and the pull-back grid builders (`rigid_exp` /
  `affine_exp` / `apply_affine` / `affine_grid`):
  [`src/nitrix/geometry/transform.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/transform.py).
- Source — the backward warp itself (`spatial_transform`):
  [`src/nitrix/geometry/grid.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/grid.py).
- Next in reading order: [the transform ladder in matrix form](transforms.qmd#sec-homogeneous),
  then [resampling / pull-back](resampling.qmd#sec-pullback-sampling) and
  [similarity metrics](similarity.qmd#sec-ssd). Sibling units:
  [geometry foundations](../geometry/foundations.qmd),
  [statistics foundations](../statistics/foundations.qmd).
